students.text: While I understand Sarah's concern, I don't necessarily agree with the idea of imposing penalties on companies for causing environmental damage. Often these companies are merely responding to market demand, and it's the consumer who ultimately drives these businesses to prioritize profits over environmental concerns. Instead, education and awareness should be the priority. The public should be encouraged to make more sustainable choices, and companies should be rewarded for taking steps towards more sustainable practices. It's important to empower both consumers and businesses to make environmentally conscious choices, rather than punishing them for past mistakes.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: Today, we'll be discussing the issue of reducing the consumption of foods and drinks that are high in sugar, salt, and fat. Some people argue that the government should impose additional taxes on these unhealthy foods and drinks to encourage people to make healthier choices. Do you agree with this approach, or do you believe that there are other ways to address this issue? Why?

students.name: Jennifer
students.text: I strongly believe that the government should impose additional taxes on unhealthy foods and drinks. The consumption of high-sugar foods and drinks is a major contributor to obesity and other health problems. By imposing taxes, the government can encourage people to make healthier choices. The revenue generated from these taxes can also be used to fund public health initiatives and awareness campaigns.

students.name: Michael
students.text: While I understand Jennifer's viewpoint, I don't believe that additional taxes are the best solution to this problem. Taxes on unhealthy foods and drinks may not be effective in deterring people from consuming them. Furthermore, such taxes may unfairly affect people who live in food deserts or who can't afford healthier food options. Instead, the government should focus on educating people about the importance of a balanced diet and promoting affordable access to healthy foods. With a combination of education and access to healthy foods, people will be able to make informed choices and adopt healthier lifestyles.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: For this week’s discussion, let’s think about the allocation of government budget to education. Many countries now lay more emphasis on improving education, then comes the discussion whether college tuition fees should be totally paid by government revenue. Here is a question for the class discussion board: do you think university fees should be completely paid by the government?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think college tuitions should be totally covered by taxes instead of charging every individual student. For some families, paying for tuition fees is still a financial burden since it keeps increasing in the past decades, so if the government can pay for it, the financial stress for some parents can be relieved, especially those from rural and remote areas.

students.name: Paul
students.text: Although I share Claire’s view that tuition fees keeps increasing, I believe that it is not reasonable for the government to pay for it. In my opinion, the government have already allocated a large amount of money to support education, such as building libraries and research centers in a lot of universities. Also, the government provides sufficient financial aids such as scholarship and fellowship for students from poor families. So, it is unnecessary for the government to pay the tuition fees for all students.

professor.name: Professor
professor.text: In the coming weeks, we will be talking about how governments should enforce public safety. We will start with transportation safety, since it accounts for a large portion of public safety. It has become a common practice for most countries to enact laws requiring drivers and passengers to buckle up, and cyclists to wear helmet. Although such measures have proven to be effective in saving lives, critics say that they erode people's freedom. Please share your opinions with the class on this question before our next class: is it justified for governments to require citizens to wear safety gears like safety belts and helmets?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I will have to say that governments and the police officers are abusing their power when they require citizens to take certain safety measures. Free will is people's most important right, and people should be entitled to use their own discretion when it comes to risk management, as long as it does not involve others, which is the case for safety belts and helmets. In addition, such measures often incur financial burden on citizens. Yes, buckling up and wearing helmets do save lives, but in very rare cases. After all, how often do people get involved in car accidents? But they are now forced to pay certain money for uncertain accidents.

students.name: Paul
students.text: I advocate human rights and free will as much as Claire, and I would like to point out a fact: survival is the primal human right. So I think it is totally rightful for governments to enforce safety measures to protect the public. Witnessing the tragic and graphic car accident scenes and imagining the huge grief suffered by the victims' families, it would be senseless, if not cruel, to attack such measures. Also, safety belts and helmets do not only save lives, they also dramatically reduce the damage done to those who do survive car accidents and hence the medical costs incurred. All in all, mandatory safety precautions bring about so many benefits, which dwarf its minimal, while necessary, right intrusion.
